findings.note: session is base64+JSON with no signature/HMAC check, so a client can forge arbitrary session content (no RCE since json is used)
requests.get on a fully user-controlled URL with no scheme/host allowlist and no timeout: SSRF plus indefinite hang
shlex.split avoids shell-metacharacter injection but the function still executes any binary/args the user supplies with no allowlist or sandboxing
password stored in plaintext in the in-memory dict despite the comment acknowledging it should be hashed
pickle.loads on attacker-controlled cookie bytes is unsafe deserialization enabling RCE
raw pickle.loads directly on the cookie string with no error handling: unsafe deserialization, RCE
falls back to pickle.loads on the decoded cookie data if JSON parsing fails: unsafe deserialization, RCE
user-supplied filename joined into the save path with no sanitization (no secure_filename/basename): path traversal to arbitrary write
unbalanced parentheses make this a syntax error / non-functional; if fixed, unsanitized user command run via subprocess would still be a concern
tarfile.extractall on an uploaded archive with no member-path validation extracts directly into /var/www/html: classic tar-slip path traversal / arbitrary file write to the web root
file.filename used verbatim to build the save path with no secure_filename/sanitization: path traversal on upload
yaml.load with no Loader argument uses the unsafe loader, allowing arbitrary object construction/code execution from user-supplied YAML
password stored as plaintext in the dict despite the comment noting it isn't secure
urlopen on a fully user-controlled URL with no validation and no timeout: SSRF plus indefinite hang
pickle.loads runs on the raw cookie before the HMAC check even executes, so unsafe deserialization gives RCE regardless of the (also non-constant-time) integrity check
requests.get on a fully user-controlled URL with no allowlist and no timeout: SSRF plus indefinite hang
query string built with % formatting directly from the request parameter: classic SQL injection
all executed queries use parameterized placeholders with values passed separately; only issues are dead/unused code and a missing conn.close()
invalid import syntax ('import hashlib asHS') and a malformed nested f-string make this non-functional; intent mixes a proper salted pbkdf2 hash with a stray unsalted sha256 digest
mismatched triple-quote delimiters are a syntax error making it non-functional; as designed it would format the raw search term into HTML unescaped (reflected XSS) if fixed
base64+JSON session with no signature/HMAC verification: forgeable session content
requests.get on a fully user-controlled URL with no allowlist and no timeout: SSRF plus indefinite hang
requests.get on a fully user-controlled URL with no validation and no timeout: SSRF plus indefinite hang
yaml.load with no Loader defaults to the unsafe loader on user-supplied YAML: arbitrary code execution risk
uses yaml.safe_load, which is the correct safe API for untrusted YAML
json.loads with no signature/integrity check on the cookie: session content is fully forgeable by the client (though safe from RCE since it's plain JSON)
uses unsalted single-round SHA-256 as the 'secure' hash (fast, no salt, brute-forceable) and the function is actually a no-op stub that never persists anything
SQL query is properly parameterized, but the login check compares the stored value directly to the plaintext password, implying/requiring plaintext password storage
parameterized query avoids SQL injection, but plaintext password comparison implies passwords are stored unhashed
base64+JSON session deserialization with no signature/HMAC check: forgeable session data
file.filename used directly to build the save path with no sanitization: path traversal on upload
username interpolated into the SQL query with % formatting (SQL injection) and password compared as plaintext
